original_hypothesis.title: Permissionless burning (burn/burnFrom) irreversibly destroys gauge shares without returning underlying, enabling grief and user fund loss
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: LiquidityGauge exposes ERC20Burnable burn() and burnFrom() inherited functions. Burning reduces gauge token balance and triggers ledger sync reductions via _afterTokenTransfer, but does not transfer underlying back, leaving underlying stuck in the gauge contract. Attackers who obtain allowance can burn others' gauge shares (burnFrom) to grief and permanently destroy their claim to underlying.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a concrete on-chain irreversible loss path. Even without allowance abuse, integrations may accidentally call burn(). With allowance, an adversary can destroy principal without needing to steal underlying.

proof_of_signal.pattern: LiquidityGauge is ERC20Burnable; _afterTokenTransfer calls LendingLedger.sync_ledger on burns, but burn/burnFrom do not return underlying; only withdrawUnderlying transfers underlying

preconditions: User holds LiquidityGauge tokens representing deposited underlying
Either (a) user mistakenly calls burn(), or (b) attacker has allowance and calls burnFrom(user, amount)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Standard ERC20Burnable interface is exposed on a wrapper token that should represent redeemable underlying

operational_questions: Are burn/burnFrom intended to exist for this wrapper token?
Do any frontends/integrations use burn accidentally?
Is there any recovery mechanism for underlying trapped after burns (none visible)?
validation_requirements: On a fork: depositUnderlying, then call burn and verify underlying remains in contract while balance decreases
Check for any documentation/UI that might call burn
Assess allowance patterns in integrations that could enable burnFrom grief

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Acquire burnFrom capability (allowance) or target self-mistake paths
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call burnFrom(victim, amount) to destroy gauge tokens
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Victim attempts withdrawUnderlying and fails due to insufficient gauge balance
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Underlying remains stranded in gauge contract permanently

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Loss of user principal and corrupted reward accounting for affected users
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Very high; requires contract migration or manual compensation
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Withdrawability of underlying; correctness of LendingLedger accounting for users

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Burn events on LiquidityGauge with no corresponding underlying transfers out
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Users reporting inability to withdraw despite underlying in gauge
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: burnFrom calls by third parties with allowances

validation_result.mechanism: unprotected ERC20Burnable burn destroys gauge shares without returning underlying
validation_result.impact_summary: Users can irreversibly lose deposited underlying tokens by calling burn() or having burnFrom() called against them, as gauge tokens are destroyed and ledger is synced but underlying tokens remain permanently stranded in the gauge contract.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: LiquidityGauge inherits ERC20Burnable (LiquidityGauge.sol:12), which exposes public burn(uint256) and burnFrom(address, uint256) functions. When burn() is called, it internally calls _burn() which reduces the user's gauge token balance and totalSupply, then triggers _afterTokenTransfer(from, address(0), amount). The _afterTokenTransfer hook correctly calls sync_ledger(from, -amount) to reduce the user's ledger balance, but crucially NO underlying tokens are transferred back to the user. Only withdrawUnderlying() performs the underlying transfer via IERC20(underlyingToken).safeTransfer(_user, _amount). After a burn, the user's gauge tokens are destroyed, their ledger position is reduced, but the underlying tokens sit permanently in the gauge contract with no recovery mechanism. This violates INV-ECON-008 (gauge totalSupply must equal underlying balance) and INV-STATE-006 (irreversible loss path).
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The defect is clearly present in the code. LiquidityGauge.sol:12 shows inheritance of ERC20Burnable. The _afterTokenTransfer override at approximately lines 55-68 handles ledger sync but has no mechanism to return underlying. The withdrawUnderlying function (lines 46-51) is the ONLY path that calls safeTransfer for underlying tokens. burn() and burnFrom() bypass this entirely.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Inheriting ERC20Burnable exposes public burn() and burnFrom() functions that are inappropriate for a wrapper token representing redeemable underlying deposits.

When burn is called (to == address(0)), from's ledger is reduced via sync_ledger but no underlying tokens are transferred back. The hook correctly syncs the ledger reduction but cannot trigger underlying token return because it's a generic hook for all transfer types.
This is the ONLY function that transfers underlying tokens back to the user. burn() and burnFrom() from ERC20Burnable call _burn() directly without any underlying transfer, creating the loss path.

calling_sequence: 3. ERC20._burn() reduces balanceOf[msg.sender] by amount, reduces totalSupply by amount
5. LiquidityGauge._afterTokenTransfer: from != address(0) is true, calls sync_ledger(from, -amount)
7. _afterTokenTransfer: to == address(0), so second sync_ledger is NOT called
8. Execution completes — gauge tokens destroyed, ledger updated, but NO underlying transferred back
9. Underlying tokens remain in gauge contract permanently with no recovery path
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The defect lies in the inheritance of ERC20Burnable at LiquidityGauge.sol:12 without overriding burn()/burnFrom() to also transfer underlying tokens. The _afterTokenTransfer hook at LiquidityGauge.sol:55-68 correctly handles ledger accounting but cannot handle underlying token returns because it's a generic hook. The withdrawUnderlying function at LiquidityGauge.sol:46-51 is the only function that pairs _burn with safeTransfer of underlying. Direct burn() calls bypass this pairing entirely.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: Deploy.sol shows standard deployment with no special configuration for LiquidityGauge. LiquidityGauge instances are created by LendingLedger.whiteListLendingMarket via `new LiquidityGauge(_market, address(this))`. No override or restriction on burn functions.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Scenario 1 (Self-grief): User with 1000 gauge tokens calls burn(1000) instead of withdrawUnderlying(1000). Their gauge balance goes to 0, ledger is synced to 0, but 1000 underlying tokens remain locked in gauge forever. Scenario 2 (Allowance grief): User A approves User B for gauge tokens (e.g., for a DEX or integration). User B calls burnFrom(A, amount), destroying A's gauge tokens. A can no longer withdraw the underlying. Scenario 3 (Integration error): A smart contract integration calls the standard ERC20Burnable burn() function on gauge tokens, permanently losing underlying deposits for its users.
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH severity. Any call to burn() or burnFrom() results in permanent, irreversible loss of underlying tokens. The underlying tokens become stranded in the gauge contract with no recovery mechanism. This affects both individual users who accidentally call burn() and users whose tokens are burned via burnFrom() by an approved spender. The defect also corrupts LendingLedger accounting, as the user loses future reward accrual for the burned position. No admin recovery mechanism exists in the contract.
recommended_mitigations: Remove ERC20Burnable inheritance from LiquidityGauge since burn functionality is already handled correctly by withdrawUnderlying
Alternatively, override burn() and burnFrom() to revert with a message directing users to use withdrawUnderlying()
If burn must be preserved, override burn(uint256) to call withdrawUnderlying(amount) internally, ensuring underlying tokens are always returned
Add a rescue function for governance to recover stranded tokens (as a defense-in-depth measure)
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy LendingLedger, GaugeController, whitelist a market with gauge. 2. User deposits 1000 underlying via depositUnderlying. 3. Verify balanceOf(user) == 1000 and underlying.balanceOf(gauge) == 1000. 4. User calls burn(1000). 5. Verify balanceOf(user) == 0, totalSupply() == 0. 6. Verify underlying.balanceOf(gauge) == 1000 (tokens stranded). 7. Verify user cannot call withdrawUnderlying(1000) (insufficient gauge balance). 8. For burnFrom: repeat with user A granting allowance to user B, B calling burnFrom(A, amount).
uncertainties: Whether any existing frontend or integration contracts currently call burn() on gauge tokens (would indicate active exploitation risk)
Whether the governance team intended ERC20Burnable for a specific purpose not documented in the code
additional_testing_needed: Verify that burn triggers correct _afterTokenTransfer behavior on a live fork
Check if any deployed integrations have approved gauge token allowances that could enable burnFrom attacks
Test edge case where burn reduces totalSupply to 0 while underlying remains, then new depositors interact with the gauge
